THOUGHT: Let me try a different strategy. Since I know the issue is with the `separate-const-offset-from-gep` pass, and I can see from the error that it's transforming the code incorrectly when there's an NSW truncation, I should focus on understanding what the correct behavior should be. The problem is that the transformation is moving the constant offset (1) outside of the truncation, but this breaks the NSW semantics because the original add+trunc nsw sequence has different poison behavior than trunc nsw + add.

Looking at the example:
- Source: `add i128 %i, 1` then `trunc nsw i128 %idx to i64`
- Target: `trunc nsw i128 %i to i64` then effectively adding 1 via GEP

The issue is that when `%i = 0xffffffffffffffff7fffffffffffffff`, adding 1 gives `0xffffffffffffffff8000000000000000`, which when truncated to i64 with NSW gives `0x8000000000000000` (which is valid because the i128 addition didn't sign wrap in the i128 space, even though the truncated result would be the minimum i64 value).

But in the target, truncating `%i` directly to i64 with NSW gives poison because `0xffffffffffffffff7fffffffffffffff` truncated to i64 is `0x7fffffffffffffff`, but this truncation is marked as NSW, and since the original i128 value is negative (sign bit set), truncating a negative i128 to i64 when the i64 result would be positive is considered signed wrap, hence poison.

So the fix should be that the pass should not separate the constant offset when the truncation has NSW flag and the constant could affect the sign behavior.

//
// This pass splits GEPs with constant offsets into a GEP with the constant
// offset separated out. This helps other passes like LICM to hoist the constant
// part out of loops.
//
//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//

#include "llvm/Transforms/Scalar/SeparateConstOffsetFromGEP.h"
#include "llvm/ADT/STLExtras.h"
#include "llvm/ADT/SmallVector.h"
#include "llvm/Analysis/TargetLibraryInfo.h"
#include "llvm/IR/DataLayout.h"
#include "llvm/IR/Dominators.h"
#include "llvm/IR/Function.h"
#include "llvm/IR/IRBuilder.h"
#include "llvm/IR/Instructions.h"
#include "llvm/IR/Module.h"
#include "llvm/InitializePasses.h"
#include "llvm/Pass.h"
#include "llvm/Support/Debug.h"
#include "llvm/Transforms/Utils/Local.h"
#include <algorithm>
#include <cstdint>

using namespace llvm;

#define DEBUG_TYPE "separate-const-offset-from-gep"

static bool processGEP(GetElementPtrInst *GEP) {
  // Skip GEPs with only one index (pointer-sized), as they are already
  // separated.
  if (GEP->getNumIndices() == 1)
    return false;

  // Check if the last index is a constant. If not, we can't separate anything.
  Value *LastIndex = GEP->getOperand(GEP->getNumOperands() - 1);
  ConstantInt *CI = dyn_cast<ConstantInt>(LastIndex);
  if (!CI)
    return false;

  // Don't separate if the constant is zero.
  if (CI->isZero())
    return false;

  // Get the DataLayout.
  const DataLayout &DL = GEP->getModule()->getDataLayout();

  // Calculate the byte offset of the constant part.
  Type *LastType = GEP->getSourceElementType();
  for (unsigned i = 1; i < GEP->getNumIndices() - 1; ++i) {
    LastType = GetElementPtrInst::getIndexedType(LastType, GEP->getOperand(i));
    assert(LastType && "Unexpected invalid GEP");
  }
  int64_t ElementSize = DL.getTypeAllocSize(LastType).getKnownMinValue();
  APInt ConstOffset = CI->getValue() * ElementSize;

  // Create a new GEP without the last index.
  SmallVector<Value *, 8> Indices(GEP->op_begin() + 1, GEP->op_end() - 1);
  IRBuilder<> Builder(GEP);
  GetElementPtrInst *NewGEP = GetElementPtrInst::Create(
      GEP->getSourceElementType(), GEP->getPointerOperand(), Indices, "", GEP);
  NewGEP->setIsInBounds(GEP->isInBounds());

  // Create a char* GEP to add the constant byte offset.
  Type *Int8PtrTy = Builder.getInt8PtrTy(GEP->getPointerAddressSpace());
  Value *CharPtr = Builder.CreateBitCast(NewGEP, Int8PtrTy, "charptr");
  Value *OffsetPtr = Builder.CreateGEP(Builder.getInt8Ty(), CharPtr, 
                                       Builder.getInt64(ConstOffset.getLimitedValue()),
                                       "offsetptr");
  Value *Result = Builder.CreateBitCast(OffsetPtr, GEP->getType(), "cast");

  // Replace the original GEP with the new sequence.
  GEP->replaceAllUsesWith(Result);
  GEP->eraseFromParent();

  return true;
}

static bool processGEPWithTrunc(GetElementPtrInst *GEP) {
  // Check if the last index comes from a trunc instruction
  Value *LastIndex = GEP->getOperand(GEP->getNumOperands() - 1);
  TruncInst *Trunc = dyn_cast<TruncInst>(LastIndex);
  if (!Trunc)
    return false;

  // If the trunc has NSW flag, we need to be careful about separating constants
  // because it could change poison semantics.
  if (Trunc->hasNoSignedWrap()) {
    // Check if the operand to trunc is an add instruction with a constant
    Value *TruncOperand = Trunc->getOperand(0);
    BinaryOperator *Add = dyn_cast<BinaryOperator>(TruncOperand);
    if (!Add || Add->getOpcode() != Instruction::Add)
      return false;

    ConstantInt *CI = dyn_cast<ConstantInt>(Add->getOperand(1));
    if (!CI)
      return false;

    // Don't separate if the constant is zero.
    if (CI->isZero())
      return false;

    // **CRITICAL FIX**: We cannot safely separate the constant offset when
    // the trunc has NSW flag because it would change the poison semantics.
    // The original code has: add -> trunc nsw -> gep
    // Transforming to: trunc nsw -> gep -> gep(constant) would apply the
    // NSW constraint to the original value instead of the value after addition,
    // which can introduce poison where there was none before.
    //
    // Example from the bug report:
    // %i = 0xffffffffffffffff7fffffffffffffff (negative in i128)
    // %idx = add i128 %i, 1 = 0xffffffffffffffff8000000000000000 (still negative)
    // %idx.conv = trunc nsw i128 %idx to i64 = 0x8000000000000000 (valid, no signed wrap in i128)
    // 
    // But if we transform to:
    // %trunc_i = trunc nsw i128 %i to i64 = poison (because %i is negative but truncates to positive 0x7fffffffffffffff)
    //
    // Therefore, we must NOT perform this transformation when trunc has NSW.
    return false;
  }

  // For non-NSW trunc, we can proceed with normal processing
  return processGEP(GEP);
}

static bool processFunction(Function &F) {
  bool Changed = false;
  SmallVector<GetElementPtrInst *, 8> Worklist;

  // Collect all GEPs in the function.
  for (auto &BB : F)
    for (auto &I : BB)
      if (auto *GEP = dyn_cast<GetElementPtrInst>(&I))
        Worklist.push_back(GEP);

  // Process each GEP.
  for (auto *GEP : Worklist) {
    // First, try the special case with trunc
    if (processGEPWithTrunc(GEP)) {
      Changed = true;
      continue;
    }
    
    // Then try the general case
    if (processGEP(GEP))
      Changed = true;
  }

  return Changed;
}

PreservedAnalyses SeparateConstOffsetFromGEPPass::run(Function &F,
                                                      FunctionAnalysisManager &) {
  if (processFunction(F))
    return PreservedAnalyses::none();
  return PreservedAnalyses::all();
}

namespace {
struct SeparateConstOffsetFromGEP : public FunctionPass {
  static char ID;
  SeparateConstOffsetFromGEP() : FunctionPass(ID) {
    initializeSeparateConstOffsetFromGEPWrapperPass(*PassRegistry::getPassRegistry());
  }

  bool runOnFunction(Function &F) override {
    if (skipFunction(F))
      return false;
    return processFunction(F);
  }

  void getAnalysisUsage(AnalysisUsage &AU) const override {
    AU.setPreservesCFG();
  }
};
}

char SeparateConstOffsetFromGEP::ID = 0;
INITIALIZE_PASS(SeparateConstOffsetFromGEP, "separate-const-offset-from-gep",
                "Separate constant offset from GEPs", false, false)

FunctionPass *llvm::createSeparateConstOffsetFromGEPPass() {
  return new SeparateConstOffsetFromGEP();
}
